Motion To Plaintiff'S Repsonse To Wells Fargo Bank, N.A.'S Amended Demand For Physical Examination Of Plaintiff
Off Calendar Oct-20-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES (VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE):
Plaintiff KHADIJAH JACOB (Tel: 415-409-1177) in pro per;
Atty. DEBRA STEEL STURMER, Esq. of Lerch Sturmer LLP (Tel: 415-217-6342) for Defendants Wells Fargo Bank, N.A. (erroneously named as Wells Fargo Bank)

HEARING RE: PLAINTIFF KHADIJAH JACOB's Repsonse To Wells Fargo Bank, N.A.'S Amended Demand For Physical Examination Of Plaintiff.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Plaintiff is ordered to appear within 5 days of the hearing for a rescheduled physical examination.

This is an action for damages for injuries claimed to have been suffered in a slip and fall accident in a Wells Fargo branch office. The case is set for trial on November 16, 2020. On July 21, 2020, Judge Pro Tem Fleming heard Defendant's motion for an order compelling Plaintiff Khadijah Jacob to comply with Defendant's demand for a physical examination, and issued a report and recommendation that the motion be granted. Plaintiff did not stipulate to the judge pro tem deciding that matter, so on August 6, 2020, this Court held a hearing on the report and recommendation. Plaintiff did not contest the Court's tentative ruling adopting that report and recommendation, and on the same day, the Court issued its order requiring Plaintiff to appear for and comply with a physical examination not less than ten days after written and email notice to Plaintiff. Plaintiff subsequently moved for reconsideration of that order, asserting that she had been unable to appear by phone or videoconference (Zoom), but Plaintiff then took her motion for reconsideration off calendar. Plaintiff now has filed a document entitled "Plaintiff's Motion Response to Wells Fargo Bank, N.A.'s Amended Demand for Physical Examination of Plaintiff" in which she explains that she took the motion for reconsideration off calendar because she "entered into a Settlement Offered" by Wells Fargo. She goes on to say, however, that after doing so, "I changed my mind." She now again objects on various grounds-several of which she did not raise before the judge pro tem-to appearing for a physical examination.

Plaintiff's "Motion Response" is procedurally defective. She did not properly notice a motion seeking appropriate relief, such as a protective order, nor does she show grounds for such relief or for reconsideration of the Court's order. In any event, none of Plaintiff's objections is meritorious. Plaintiff does not need access to a law library in order to attend a physical examination; Wells Fargo has voluntarily limited the scope of the examination to her claimed right shoulder injury; it is entitled to a physical examination in order to assess Plaintiff's claimed injuries even if she has already responded to written discovery requests on the same subject; and by filing suit, Plaintiff put her physical condition in controversy and thereby waived the physician-patient privilege and her right to medical privacy to that extent. (See Code Civ. Proc. Sec. 2032.250.)

In short, Wells Fargo has a statutory right to conduct the examination in order to prepare for the upcoming trial, and Plaintiff has an obligation to comply with the Court's order and rules, notwithstanding her self-represented status. (See, e.g., ViaView, Inc. v. Retzlaff, Inc. (2016) 1 Cal.App.5th 198, 208 [self-represented litigants must follow correct rules of procedure].) If Plaintiff does not appear for a rescheduled examination within 5 days of the hearing, the Court will entertain a motion for appropriate issue, evidentiary and/or terminating sanctions.

Defense counsel to prepare and submit a proposed order.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Sean Kane; Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR No. 8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com (302/EPS)

RULING - A R G U E D. The Court announces the tentative ruling and cites a case. Plaintiff argues punitive damages, conscious disregard, non-intentional act. Defendant responds. (as fully reported in the reporter's notes/transcript) The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

DEFENDANT SERGIY VLADYCHKIN, WELLS FARGO BANK, N.A.'s Motion To Strike Claim For Punitive Damages Alleged In First Amended Complaint.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:Defendants Wells Fargo Bank, N.A. and Branch Manager, Sergiy Vladychkin's motion to strike claim for punitive damages alleged in First Amended Complaint is granted without leave to amend.

In order to state a prima facie claim for punitive damages, a complaint must set forth the elements stated in the general punitive damage statute, Civil Code section 3294, including allegations that the defendant has been guilty of oppression, fraud, or malice. (Civ. Code 3294(a).) "Malice" is defined as conduct "intended by the defendant to cause injury to plaintiff, or despicable conduct that is carried on by the defendant with a willful and conscious disregard for the rights or safety of others." (Civ. Code 3294(c)(1).) Absent an intent to injure the plaintiff, malice requires more than a "willful and conscious" disregard of the plaintiff's interests; the additional component of "despicable conduct" must be found. (College Hospital, Inc. v. Superior Court (1994) 8 Cal.4th 704, 725.) Punitive damages are appropriate if the defendant's actions are reprehensible, fraudulent, or in blatant violation of law or policy. The mere carelessness or ignorance of the defendant does not justify the imposition of punitive damages. Punitive damages are proper only when the tortious conduct rises to levels of extreme indifference to the plaintiff's rights, a level which decent citizens should not have to tolerate. (Pacific Gas and Electric Company v. Superior Court (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 1150, 1170-76.)

This is a slip and fall action in which Plaintiff charges defendant with negligence and premises liability for failing to repair a loose rug and floor mats and to warn customers of a dangerous condition they posed. Such conduct does not rise to the level of despicable conduct carried on by the defendant with a willful and conscious disregard for the rights or safety of others required by Civ. Code 3294(c)(1). At most, Plaintiff's allegations show "mere carelessness or ignorance, which is insufficient to establish malice." (Pacific Gas and Electric Company, 24 Cal.App.5th at 1171; see Lackner v. North (2006) 135 Cal.App.4th 1188, 1209-1210]; see also Brousseau v. Jarrett (1977) 73 Cal.App.3d 864, 872 ["conclusory characterization of defendant's conduct as intentional, willful and fraudulent is a patently insufficient statement of 'oppression, fraud, or malice, express or implied,' within the meaning of section 3294."].)

Defendant's Counsel to submit a proposed order that is verbatim with the Court's ruling in compliance with CRC 3.1312.
